elements.sent-2411-001: Jiawei Zhang* \IEEEauthorblockA{ University of Science and Technology Beijing Beijing, China javezone2@gmail.com \and \IEEEauthorblockN{2\textsuperscript{nd Tian-Hao Zhang \IEEEauthorblockA{ University of Science and Technology Beijing Beijing, China tianhaozhang@xs.ustb.edu.cn \and \IEEEauthorblockN{3\textsuperscript{rd Jun Wang \IEEEauthorblockA{Tencent AI Lab Shenzhen, China junjunmin@gmail.com \linebreakand \IEEEauthorblockN{4\textsuperscript{th Jiaran Gao \IEEEauthorblockA{ University of Science and Technology Beijing Beijing, China U202241904@xs.ustb.edu.cn \and \IEEEauthorblockN{5\textsuperscript{th Xinyuan Qian \IEEEauthorblockA{ University of Science and Technology Beijing Beijing, China qianxy@ustb.edu.cn \and \IEEEauthorblockN{6\textsuperscript{th Xu-Cheng Yin \IEEEauthorblockA{ University of Science and Technology Beijing Beijing, China xuchengyin@ustb.edu.cn.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Controlling the style and characteristics of speech synthesis is crucial for adapting the output to specific contexts and user requirements.
elements.sent-2411-003: Previous \ac{TTS works have focused primarily on the technical aspects of producing natural-sounding speech, such as intonation, rhythm, and clarity.}
elements.sent-2411-004: However, they overlook the fact that there is a growing emphasis on spatial perception of synthesized speech, which may provide immersive experience in gaming and virtual reality.
elements.sent-2411-005: To solve this issue, in this paper, we present a novel multi-modal \ac{TTS approach, namely \ac{ITTS .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, we introduce a scene prompt encoder that integrates visual scene prompts directly into the synthesis pipeline to control the speech generation process.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we propose a reverberation classification and refinement technique that adjusts the synthesized mel-spectrogram to enhance the immersive experience, ensuring that the involved reverberation condition matches the scene accurately.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experimental results demonstrate that our model achieves high-quality scene and spatial matching without compromising speech naturalness, marking a significant advancement in the field of context-aware speech synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-010: Speech synthesis has seen remarkable advancements in recent years, driven by breakthroughs in deep learning and neural network architectures~\cite{van2016wavenet,wang2017tacotron,ren2019fastspeech,kim2021conditional,wang2023neural .}
elements.sent-2411-011: These advances have enabled the generation of high-quality, natural-sounding speech in a variety of applications, ranging from virtual assistants to accessibility technologies.
elements.sent-2411-012: In recent years, many studies~\cite{casanova2022yourtts,guo2023prompttts,yang2024instructtts,guan2024mm have focused on using some prompts to control the emotions and characteristics of speech and synthesize specific spoken contents.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, while significant progress has been made in generating clear and intelligible speech, the ability to synthesize speech that accurately reflects the acoustic environment of a given scene remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-014: The way sound reverberates in real-world environments is crucial to how we perceive speech~\cite{stan2002comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Reverberation in a room can significantly affect the way words are understood and the overall feeling conveyed by the speech~\cite{savioja2015overview .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, most current speech synthesis models are designed for neutral or non-reverberant environments, which may not be ideal for applications requiring a more immersive auditory experience.
elements.sent-2411-017: Although some research has been able to predict the reverberation of sound using scene images~\cite{singh2021image2reverb, chen2022visual, majumder2022few, chen2023learning, somayazulu2024self , direct synthesis of reverberant speech is still an ongoing challenge.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To address the aforementioned issues, in our paper, we present an innovative multi-modal \ac{TTS task, i.e., the \ac{ITTS .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: This task involves creating speech that not only communicates the intended message but also includes reverberation characteristics that match the acoustic properties of a specific visual scene.
elements.sent-2411-020: Specifically, our approach utilizes prompts from image scenes to synthesize speech, aiming to provide a more immersive and realistic auditory experience.
elements.sent-2411-021: Unlike traditional \ac{TTS works, our method integrates reverberation as an essential part of the synthesis process rather than treating it as an optional post-processing effect~\cite{singh2021image2reverb .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{pipeline.pdf} \caption{Overview of our proposed multi-modal immersive \ac{TTS} method given the scene image as the environment indicator.} \label{fig:pipline}
elements.sent-2411-022: In order to tackle this challenge, we have integrated scene-aware reverberation directly into the speech synthesis process.
elements.sent-2411-023: To capture the acoustic characteristics of a given environment, we first use an image encoder to extract visual features from the scene's image.
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, we employ the \ac{CLIP ~\cite{radford2021learning model for our image encoding process, as it effectively aligns visual and textual information, enabling the model to extract meaningful features representing the scene's environment.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Once we obtain the scene representation from the image encoder, we incorporate it into the speech synthesis process.
elements.sent-2411-026: The entire pipeline is built on \ac{VITS ~\cite{kim2021conditional , with modifications to incorporate the scene-aware features.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Once the mel-spectrogram is generated, we further refine it using a \ac{SRC model, which adjusts the reverberation to ensure that it matches the scene.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our work outlines the motivation behind this new task, describes the methodology for incorporating scene prompts into speech synthesis, and presents experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in generating contextually appropriate immersive speech.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-030: We introduce the first multi-modal \ac{TTS model with controllable reverberation effects based on the visual scene provided as a prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-031: This approach allows for precise control over the acoustic characteristics of the synthesized speech, making it highly adaptable to different environments.
elements.sent-2411-032: We have developed a new network including a \ac{SPE to extract scene image features, a \ac{SRC to refine the reverberation of the synthesized speech, and a \ac{TTS backbone based on \ac{VITS that maps the given text to immersive speech.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Both subjective and objective experiments demonstrate that our proposed method can generate high-fidelity speech which is spatially aligned with characteristics depicted by a scene image.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{method.pdf} \caption{Block diagram of the \ac{ITTS} model ($\oplus$ indicates element-wise addition).} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-034: This section reviews key developments in \ac{TTS , with a focus on areas relevant to our proposed task: reverberation modeling, context-aware speech synthesis, and the integration of visual cues in speech generation.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Early speech synthesis systems, such as formant synthesis and concatenative synthesis, focused on generating speech by stringing together pre-recorded phonemes or sound units.
elements.sent-2411-036: Although these methods were able to produce intelligible speech, they often lacked naturalness and flexibility, particularly in varying acoustic environments.
elements.sent-2411-037: The advent of deep learning brought about a paradigm shift, with models like WaveNet~\cite{van2016wavenet and Tacotron~\cite{wang2017tacotron that enable end-to-end TTS to produce highly natural and expressive speech.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Since then, TTS has entered an explosive development with many models being explored, such as FastSpeech2~\cite{ren2020fastspeech , Glow-TTS~\cite{kim2020glow , Diff-TTS~\cite{jeong2021diff , VITS and ProDiff~\cite{huang2022prodiff .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Recently, discrete token-based audio synthesis approaches, i.e. UniCATS~\cite{du2024unicats , CLaM-TTS~\cite{kim2023clam , VALL-E~\cite{wang2023neural , AudioLM~\cite{borsos2023audiolm and NaturalSpeech3~\cite{ju2024naturalspeech formulate the generation process as a conditional language modeling task.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Leveraging extensive training data and the power of large language models, they notably enhance speech quality and naturalness.
elements.sent-2411-041: However, these models typically generate speech in a neutral and non-reverberant environment, without considering the acoustic characteristics of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-042: Context-aware speech synthesis aims to generate speech that is sensitive to various contextual factors, such as the speaker's emotional state, the intended audience, or the environmental setting.
elements.sent-2411-043: Emotional speech synthesis~\cite{schroder2001emotional , for example, has received significant attention, with models trained to produce speech that reflects specific emotions.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Similarly, prosody modeling~\cite{ren2020fastspeech,huang2022prodiff has been explored to adjust the rhythm, intonation, and stress patterns of speech based on context.}
elements.sent-2411-045: However, the consideration of environmental acoustics, particularly reverberation, in response to visual scene context remains largely unexplored in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-046: Reverberation modeling has traditionally been addressed in the field of audio signal processing, where techniques such as convolution with room \ac{RIR are used to simulate the effects of different acoustic environments on speech.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the context of speech synthesis, reverberation is often applied as a post-processing step, where the synthesized speech is convolved with an \ac{RIR to achieve the desired effect~\cite{singh2021image2reverb, chen2022visual, somayazulu2024self .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Although effective, this approach treats reverberation as an integral part of the synthesis process rather than an afterthought.
elements.sent-2411-049: Recent work has begun to explore the integration of reverberation directly within the speech generation pipeline, but these efforts have been limited in scope and typically do not consider the scene context.
elements.sent-2411-050: The overall architecture of our proposed framework is shown in Fig~\ref{fig:method (a), which consists of three modules: 1) a \ac{CLIP -based image encoder and an adapter to extract scene-specific features and align the features to a speech style space; 2) a speech reverberation model to classify and refine the synthesized mel-spectrogram, ensuring that the reverberation matches the visual scene; 3) a \ac{VITS -based \ac{TTS backbone that maps the given text to reverberant speech.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Our proposal is expected to generate speech that not only sounds natural, but also creates a sense of spatial presence driven by the scene prompt.
elements.sent-2411-052: In order to capture the acoustic characteristics of a given scene prompt, we first utilize a scene prompt encoder based on the \ac{CLIP model and an adapter to extract visual features from the scene image that represent the scene's likely acoustic properties.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus supporting flexible scene control speech generation.
elements.sent-2411-054: The adapter module in our model is designed to efficiently integrate visual features of the scene into the speech synthesis pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-055: This module is simply comprised of a \ac{GeLU activation function followed by two fully connected layers.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In practice, we first input the scene image $I_{i}$ into the \ac{CLIP -based image encoder to extract the scene prompt features.}
elements.sent-2411-057: After being fed to the adapter, we transform the scene prompt features into an acoustic space embedding $I_{e}$.
elements.sent-2411-058: To make this embedding close to the reverb category $r_{c}$, we guide it with a \ac{CE loss function, which is computed as follows: \begin{equation}L_{s}= \text{CrossEntropy}\left ( I_{e}, r_{c}\right )\end{equation} Once obtaining the output embedding from the adapter module, we directly feed it into the \ac{TTS backbone.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: This integration allows the scene-aware information, encapsulated in the embedding, to influence various stages of the \ac{TTS pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-060: To ensure that the reverberation characteristics of the synthesized speech are accurately matched to the visual scene, once the initial mel-spectrogram is generated, we further refine it using an \ac{SRC model that classifies and adjusts the reverberation to ensure that it matches the scene.}
elements.sent-2411-061: For the architecture illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:method (b), the \ac{SRC reveives the mel-spectrogram from the synthesis speech and predicts the class of reverberation.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Specifically, the main network comprises four 2-D convolution blocks to capture local time-frequency features and a transformer~\cite{vaswani2017attention block to obtain global dependencies across time frames.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Then the features of the two blocks are concatenated to form a comprehensive feature representation fed into a softmax layer for further transformation, followed by a classification layer that outputs the final predictions.
elements.sent-2411-064: The predictions of the reverb class $r_{p}$ are finally computed by a \ac{CE loss as follows: \begin{equation}L_{m}= \text{CrossEntropy}\left ( r_{p}, r_{c}\right )\end{equation} The \ac{SRC model is trained to classify different types of reverberation based on the combined input of scene features and the initial synthesized mel-spectrogram.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: By analyzing these inputs, the \ac{SRC model determines the appropriate reverberation profile that should be present in the speech for the given scene.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In addition to classifying the reverberation, the \ac{SRC model further adjusts the mel-spectrogram accordingly.}
elements.sent-2411-067: This refinement process involves fine-tuning the reverberation effects embedded in the mel-spectrogram to ensure that they align with the acoustic properties suggested by the scene.
elements.sent-2411-068: Adjustments may involve modifying the duration, intensity, or other characteristics of reverb to achieve a more accurate match.
elements.sent-2411-069: Backbone The \ac{VITS model serves as the backbone of our speech synthesis process, which is known for its ability to produce high-quality and natural speech through variational inference.}
elements.sent-2411-070: It comprises several key components that work together to generate high-quality speech synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-071: First, the Posterior Encoder and Text Encoder are responsible for generating the posterior distribution and prior distribution, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-072: To further refine the latent representation produced by the Posterior Encoder, the model incorporates a Transformer-based Flow module.
elements.sent-2411-073: Additionally, the model employs a \ac{MAS module, which is crucial for estimating the alignment between the input text and the target speech, ensuring that the synthesized speech correctly matches the timing and sequence of the phonemes in the input text.}
elements.sent-2411-074: The Duration Predictor module is another critical component, tasked with predicting the duration of each phoneme.
elements.sent-2411-075: Finally, the Decoder takes the refined latent representation produced by the Posterior Encoder and reconstructs the speech waveform.
elements.sent-2411-076: During the synthesis process, the inputs to the \ac{VITS model include the textual content to be synthesized and the scene feature vector obtained from the \ac{CLIP model to facilitate the generation of speech that effectively incorporates the appropriate reverberation effects for the scene.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Finally, the \ac{SRC model assesses whether the reverberation embedded in the generated mel-spectrogram aligns with the expected acoustic environment based on the scene features, ensuring that the final output speech not only accurately represents the content but also incorporates the acoustic properties of the scene.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The total loss can be computed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:loss} L_{total}= \lambda_{1}L_{VITS} + \lambda_{2}L_{s} + \lambda_{3}L_{m}\end{equation} where $\lambda_1$, $\lambda_2$ and $\lambda_3$ are the hyperparameters to adjust the contributions of each loss component.
elements.sent-2411-079: For our experiments, we used two primary datasets: LJSpeech~\cite{ito2017lj and Image2Reverb~\cite{singh2021image2reverb .}}
elements.sent-2411-080: These datasets provide the necessary diversity in speech, reverberated sound, and scene images to train and evaluate our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-081: Specifically, we randomly convolve the clean speech data from the LJSpeech dataset with impulse responses from the Image2Reverb dataset.
elements.sent-2411-082: This process generates convolutional speech samples with reverberation effects that correspond to the visual scenes depicted in the images, which finally serves as the ground truth for training, providing a benchmark for evaluating the accuracy and naturalness of the synthesized output.
elements.sent-2411-083: Additionally, we pretrained the \ac{SRC model on the convolutional speech samples over 300 epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Once integrated into the \ac{TTS framework, we froze its parameters and only used the model to calculate the classification loss of the generated speech.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The \ac{TTS framework was trained for 200K iterations using AdamW optimizer on 3 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The initial learning rate was established at 1e-4 and the batch size for \ac{TTS training is 24.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The hyperparameters in Eq.~\ref{eq:loss are set to 10, 2 and 1, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-088: As there is no related work for scene-driven immersive speech synthesis, we utilize \ac{VITS endowed with a scene prompt and train it on the same \ac{RIR -convolved speech samples as our baseline system.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Specifically, we conducted experiments on the following systems: 1) GT: the ground-truth convolutional speech.
elements.sent-2411-090: To assess the effectiveness of our proposed method, we first evaluate the quality of the generated speech and the correspondence between the scene and the reverberation using some objective metrics including \ac{MCD ~\cite{kubichek1993mel and \ac{SRE .}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: It is computed by comparing the \ac{MFCCs of the synthesized speech with those of the reference speech.}
elements.sent-2411-092: A lower \ac{MCD value indicates that the synthesized speech is closer to the ground truth in terms of spectral characteristics, implying higher quality.}
elements.sent-2411-093: A lower \ac{SRE indicates that the spatial characteristics of the synthesized speech closely match those of the target scene, demonstrating effective scene-reverberation alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-094: We also conduct subjective evaluations to assess perceptual aspects that are difficult to capture quantitatively.
elements.sent-2411-095: We use a \ac{MOS ~\cite{sisman2020overview with 95 $\%$ confidence intervals to assess speech quality, where listeners are asked to rate the audio on a scale from 1 to 5.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: We utilize the \ac{CMOS to ascertain the clarity of synthesized speech, enabling us to assess the quality of pronunciation and the ease of word recognition by listeners.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Simultaneously, we employ the \ac{NMOS to gauge the naturalness of synthesized speech to human listeners, evaluating its overall fluidity, expressiveness, and resemblance to human speech.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Furthermore, We test \ac{IMOS for matching between scene prompt and spatial characteristics of speech to evaluate the effectiveness of our model in aligning with human perceptions of the scene's acoustics.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Objective evaluation results.} \begin{tabular}{cccccc} Method MCD $\downarrow$ SRE $\downarrow$ GT - 0.214 Baseline 4.01 0.782 Proposed method w/o SR Classifier 4.29 0.397 Proposed method 4.22 0.272 \end{tabular} \label{tab:obj}
elements.sent-2411-099: Table~\ref{tab:obj displays the objective evaluation results.}
elements.sent-2411-100: By incorporating the SR Classifier, we significantly improve the alignment between the scene and the reverberation effects in the synthesized speech.
elements.sent-2411-101: Our proposed method achieves an MCD of 4.22, a figure close to the baseline.
elements.sent-2411-102: However, the SRE of our proposed method is far superior to the baseline and closely aligns with the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-103: The objective metrics results show that our approach not only enhances the scene-reverberation alignment but also maintains speech quality.
elements.sent-2411-104: Table~\ref{tab:sbj presents the subjective evaluation results.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Without any proposed modules, the baseline model has a \ac{IMOS of 3.28, much lower than other models.}
elements.sent-2411-106: In contrast, Our model excels with the second-highest scores across all subjective metrics, just behind the GT.
elements.sent-2411-107: This demonstrates that it surpasses the baseline models in terms of reverberation accuracy, speech clarity and speech naturalness.
elements.sent-2411-108: These results highlight the effectiveness of our method in scene-aware reverberation matching while ensuring the clarity and high quality of the generated speech.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{subjective evaluation results.} \begin{tabular}{cccccc} Method CMOS $\uparrow$ NMOS $\uparrow$ IMOS $\uparrow$ GT 4.57 4.54 4.63 Baseline 3.75 3.92 3.28 Proposed method w/o SR Classifier 3.72 3.77 3.81 Proposed method 3.87 3.98 4.06 \end{tabular} \label{tab:sbj}
elements.sent-2411-109: To evaluate the performance of our \ac{SRC model, we track \ac{SRE in different training epochs.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:RE , the \ac{SRE reduces steadily with each epoch, reflecting that the \ac{SRC model becomes more accurate in classifying and refining the reverberation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The improvement plateauing in later epochs suggests that the model converges effectively, consistently improving the matching degree between scene and reverberation as training progresses.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{SRE.pdf} \caption{The \ac{SRE} of our \ac{SRC} model with increasing training epoch.} \label{fig:RE}
elements.sent-2411-112: To gain insight into which visual features are most influential in our encoder, we employ \ac{Grad-CAM ~\cite{selvaraju2017grad , a widely used technique applied to visually interpret the image regions that contribute the most to scene understanding.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: We produce such maps for our test images with the scene prompt encoder as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:grad-cam .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The original images are shown on the left, with the corresponding \ac{Grad-CAM heatmaps on the right.}
elements.sent-2411-115: These heat maps highlight areas the model considers crucial for extracting image features relevant to the acoustic environment.
elements.sent-2411-116: The color scale, which ranges from blue to red, indicates increasing levels of importance.
elements.sent-2411-117: For example, in the wide environment (A), the highlighted regions emphasize broad structural elements such as walls and seating arrangements.
elements.sent-2411-118: In contrast, in the narrow environment (B), attention is focused on confined linear features such as hallways.
elements.sent-2411-119: Experimental results demonstrate that the detected visual objects aid in \ac{TTS systems with reverberation.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{gradcam.pdf} \caption{\ac{Grad-CAM}s for images passed the scene prompt encoder, showing movement towards more textured areas for (A) a wide, and (B) a narrow environment.} \label{fig:grad-cam}
elements.sent-2411-120: In this work, we introduced a novel approach to speech synthesis that embeds scene-aware reverberation into the synthesis process, resulting in speech that not only matches the text, but also aligns with the acoustic properties of the visual scene.
elements.sent-2411-121: Our method represents a significant advancement in the field by enabling controllable reverberant speech synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-122: Furthermore, our reverberation classification and refinement mechanism ensures that the synthesized speech is both perceptually and contextually appropriate for the given scene.
elements.sent-2411-123: We demonstrated that our model achieves high-quality scene and reverb matching without deteriorating the naturalness of the speech.
elements.sent-2411-124: The combination of objective metrics and subjective evaluations shows that our model successfully balances speech quality with scene awareness, paving the way for more immersive and context-sensitive applications in virtual environments, interactive media, and other fields requiring spatially-aware audio.
elements.sent-2411-125: Controlling the spatial and stylistic characteristics of synthesized speech is essential for immersive and personalized applications such as virtual reality, gaming, and human-computer interaction.
elements.sent-2411-126: While recent \ac{TTS systems have explored multi-modal conditioning, they often suffer from poor reverberation fidelity or degraded audio quality due to reliance on external vocoders.}
elements.sent-2411-127: In this paper, we propose \ac{ITTS , an end-to-end multi-modal \ac{TTS framework that synthesizes high-quality, immersive speech from text and visual scene prompts.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Our model leverages a CLIP-based image encoder with an adaptive adapter to extract scene-aware features, a Speech Reverberation Classifier (SRC) for refining acoustic-visual alignment during training, and a speaker encoder to enable zero-shot speaker generalization.
elements.sent-2411-129: Built upon a VITS backbone, \ac{ITTS generates reverberant speech directly without requiring a separate vocoder.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Experimental results demonstrate that our approach produces spatially accurate and natural-sounding speech, achieving superior performance in both subjective and objective evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-131: Speech synthesis has seen remarkable advancements in recent years, driven by breakthroughs in deep learning and neural network architectures~\cite{wang2017tacotron,ren2019fastspeech,kim2021conditional,wang2023neural .}
elements.sent-2411-132: These innovations have enabled the generation of high-quality, natural-sounding speech across various applications, such as virtual assistants, accessibility technologies, and interactive media.
elements.sent-2411-133: Recently, numerous studies~\cite{casanova2022yourtts,guo2023prompttts,yang2024instructtts,guan2024mm have explored the use of prompts to control specific speech characteristics, such as emotion, style, or speaker identity, enabling personalized and dynamic speech synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Reverberation is also a critical auditory feature that defines how sound interacts with its environment, profoundly influences the perception and intelligibility of speech~\cite{stan2002comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-135: While reverberation plays a vital role in creating immersive auditory experiences, most existing speech synthesis models are designed for neutral or non-reverberant environments, limiting their applicability in spatially aware or immersive contexts.
elements.sent-2411-136: To address this, recent efforts have begun integrating environmental context into the synthesis process.
elements.sent-2411-137: For instance, Environment-Aware TTS~\cite{tan2021environment introduces an environment embedding extractor that learns environmental features from reference speech, improving the synthesis of speech tailored to specific acoustic settings by reducing intra-environment embedding distance and increasing inter-environment separation.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Similarly, VoiceLDM~\cite{lee2024voiceldm focus on mapping textual or audio descriptions into environmental feature vectors, effectively controlling the reverberation aspects of the generated audio.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Furthermore, ViT-TTS~\cite{liu2023vit presents a groundbreaking multi-modal \ac{TTS task that generates speech with reverberation characteristics closely aligned with the acoustic properties of specific visual scenes.}}
elements.sent-2411-140: This innovation marks a significant advancement by integrating visual context into TTS.
elements.sent-2411-141: In addition, MS2KU-VTTS~\cite{he2025multi explores immersive TTS by leveraging multi-source spatial knowledge, including image, depth, and semantic segmentation, to enhance spatial understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-142: However, they depend on an external vocoder for waveform generation, which introduces synthesis artifacts, such as distortions and noise, and struggles to preserve fine-grained acoustic details like reverberation.
elements.sent-2411-143: Given that reverberation is inherently linked to environmental context, achieving robust and natural reverberation modeling necessitates a more integrated and end-to-end approach within the TTS pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-144: To address this challenge, we have integrated scene-aware reverberation directly into the end-to-end speech synthesis process.
elements.sent-2411-145: In addition to scene understanding, personalization is also crucial for immersive experience.
elements.sent-2411-146: We introduce a speaker encoder module that enables zero-shot speaker adaptation, allowing the model to synthesize speech with the voice characteristics of an unseen speaker based on a short reference speech.
elements.sent-2411-147: This module extracts speaker embeddings from reference speech and conditions the speech generation process accordingly, ensuring that the synthesized output reflects the target speaker identity.
elements.sent-2411-148: The entire pipeline is built on \ac{VITS ~\cite{kim2021conditional , with modifications to incorporate the features that are relevant to the scene.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Once immersive speech is generated, we further refine it using a \ac{SRC model, which adjusts the reverberation and refines the reverberation characteristics to ensure accurate alignment with the prompt scene.}
elements.sent-2411-150: We propose the first end-to-end multi-modal \ac{TTS framework that synthesizes immersive and high-quality reverberant speech directly from text and visual scene prompts, built upon a VITS-based backbone without requiring a separately trained vocoder.}
elements.sent-2411-151: We introduce a novel architecture that includes a CLIP-based image encoder to extract scene-aware visual representations and an SRC (Scene Relevance Classifier) module to refine the acoustic consistency between the synthesized speech and the visual environment, thereby improving scene precision.
elements.sent-2411-152: Our system supports zero-shot speaker adaptation through a speaker encoder, enabling personalized and spatially grounded speech generation that realizes specific speakers in specific scenes.
elements.sent-2411-153: Extensive objective and subjective evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating spatially aligned, high-fidelity speech across diverse visual environments.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{method.png} \caption{Block diagram of the \ac{ITTS} model ($\oplus$ indicates element-wise addition).} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-154: The advent of deep learning brought about a paradigm shift, with models like Tacotron~\cite{wang2017tacotron that enables end-to-end TTS to produce highly natural and expressive speech.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Since then, TTS has entered an explosive development with many models being explored, such as FastSpeech2~\cite{ren2020fastspeech , Diff-TTS~\cite{jeong2021diff and ProDiff~\cite{huang2022prodiff .}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Recently, discrete token-based audio synthesis approaches, such as CLaM-TTS~\cite{kim2023clam , VALL-E~\cite{wang2023neural , AudioLM~\cite{borsos2023audiolm and NaturalSpeech3~\cite{ju2024naturalspeech formulate the generation process as a conditional language modeling task.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Using extensive training data and the power of large language models, they significantly enhance speech quality and naturalness.
elements.sent-2411-158: To address this limitation, recent research has started to integrate environmental cues directly into the synthesis pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-159: For instance, ViT-TTS~\cite{liu2023vit introduces a multimodal speech synthesis paradigm where visual scenes are used to condition the generation of speech with appropriate reverberation.}
elements.sent-2411-160: By leveraging visual prompts, it attempts to model the spatial context inherent in the image.
elements.sent-2411-161: Extending this direction, MS2KU-VTTS~\cite{he2025multi takes a more holistic approach to immersive TTS by incorporating multi-source spatial information, including RGB images, depth maps, and semantic segmentations.}
elements.sent-2411-162: However, due to their reliance on external vocoders, they may suffer from synthesis artifacts and limited control over fine-grained reverberation.
elements.sent-2411-163: While speech synthesis has advanced significantly in producing natural and expressive outputs, the integration of environmental and spatial context remains a relatively nascent area.
elements.sent-2411-164: Post-processing methods for spatial effects, such as RIR convolution, have been standard but lack deep integration with the synthesis process.
elements.sent-2411-165: Recent efforts, have begun to explore multi-modal approaches that utilize scene prompts, yet challenges in speech quality and acoustic alignment persist due to their reliance on external vocoders and limited reverberation modeling fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-166: Our approach addresses these limitations by tightly integrating scene-aware reverberation into the TTS pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-167: By treating environmental acoustics as an integral part of synthesis rather than a post-processing effect, our model achieves superior spatial alignment and speech quality, offering a more immersive and contextually accurate auditory experience.
elements.sent-2411-168: The overall architecture of our proposed framework is shown in Fig~\ref{fig:method .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Our proposal is expected to generate speech that not only sounds natural, but also conveys a strong sense of spatial presence and speaker identity, guided by the scene and speaker prompts.
elements.sent-2411-170: In order to capture the acoustic characteristics of a given scene prompt, we first utilize the \ac{CLIP model and an adapter to extract visual features from the scene image that represent the scene's likely acoustic properties.}
elements.sent-2411-171: This module is simply comprised of a MLP network.
elements.sent-2411-172: To make this embedding close to the reverb category $r_{c}$, we guide it with a SoftMax layer and compute a \ac{CE loss function with $r_{c}$.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Once obtaining the output embedding from the adapter module, we directly feed it into the \ac{TTS backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-174: To enable zero-shot speaker adaptation and ensure that the synthesized speech maintains consistent speaker identity, we introduce a dedicated speaker encoder module.
elements.sent-2411-175: This module extracts speaker embeddings from a short reference speech, allowing our model to imitate unseen speaker voices without retraining.
elements.sent-2411-176: Concretely, inspired by YourTTS~\cite{casanova2022yourtts , we adopt the StyleEncoder architecture from~\cite{min2021meta to encode speaker-specific characteristics into a compact embedding $S_{e}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: This embedding is then projected via a linear layer and injected into the \ac{TTS backbone through adaptive layer modulation, allowing the system to synthesize speech in the voice of an unseen speaker in a zero-shot manner.}
elements.sent-2411-178: The predictions of the reverb class $r_{p}$ are finally computed by a \ac{CE loss.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Backbone We adopt \ac{VITS ~\cite{kim2021conditional as the backbone of our system for its end-to-end design and high-quality output.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: It consists of a Posterior Encoder, Text Encoder, and Transformer-based flow for expressive latent modeling, with MAS and Duration Predictor for alignment and phoneme timing, and a Decoder to reconstruct the final waveform.
elements.sent-2411-181: During training, the model receives text, immersive speech, speaker embedding, and scene embedding as input.
elements.sent-2411-182: The scene embedding, extracted from the CLIP-based image encoder and adapted via an MLP, guides the model to learn scene-aware reverberation characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-183: Moreover, speaker embedding $S_e$, obtained via a speaker encoder, allows the system to generalize to unseen speakers in a zero-shot fashion.
elements.sent-2411-184: Both embeddings are injected into the model through conditional modules, enabling joint learning of content, speaker identity, and spatial acoustics.
elements.sent-2411-185: Finally, the \ac{SRC module verifies and refines the reverberation characteristics in the mel-spectrogram to better align with the input scene.}
elements.sent-2411-186: At inference time, the system takes input text, an image prompt, and an optional speaker reference.
elements.sent-2411-187: The adapted CLIP feature and speaker embedding modulate the synthesis process, allowing the decoder to generate immersive speech with appropriate reverberation and speaker identity.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Experimental results for different models.} \label{tab:results} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{12pt} \setlength{\textwidth}{16pt} 1.0{ \begin{tabular}{c|cccc|ccc} ~ \multicolumn{4}{c|}{Objective metrics} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Subjective metrics} Model WER(\%) $\downarrow$ MCD $\downarrow$ SECS$\uparrow$ SRE(\%) $\downarrow$ NMOS $\uparrow$ SMOS$\uparrow$ IMOS $\uparrow$ GT 4.7 / 0.76 21.4 4.54 4.46 4.63 Baseline 7.8 4.25 / 31.6 3.83 / 3.92 ViT-TTS 8.6 4.58 / 64.2 3.77 / 3.71 MS2KU-VTTS 8.2 4.49 / 57.1 3.79 / 3.86 Proposed method w/o SR Classifier 8.0 4.29 0.53 39.7 3.82 4.16 3.81 Proposed method w/o CLIP 7.8 4.27 0.57 30.8 3.85 4.19 3.83 Proposed 7.6 4.22 0.62 27.2 3.98 4.21 3.96 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-188: For our experiments, we used three primary datasets: LJSpeech~\cite{ito2017lj , VCTK~\cite{veaux2017cstr and Image2Reverb~\cite{singh2021image2reverb .}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Specifically, we randomly convolve the clean speech data from the LJSpeech dataset and VCTK dataset with impulse responses from the Image2Reverb dataset.
elements.sent-2411-190: For the comparative analysis, we conducted experiments on the following systems: 1) GT: the ground-truth convolutional speech.
elements.sent-2411-191: To assess the effectiveness of our proposed method, we first evaluate the quality of the generated speech and the correspondence between the scene and the reverberation using some objective metrics including Word Error Rate (WER), \ac{MCD ~\cite{kubichek1993mel , Speaker Encoder Cosine Similarity (SECS)~\cite{casanova2021sc and \ac{SRE .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: WER measures the accuracy of the synthesized speech in terms of word recognition.
elements.sent-2411-193: SECS is used to assess the speaker similarity between the input and generated speech.
elements.sent-2411-194: We employ the \ac{NMOS to gauge the naturalness of synthesized speech to human listeners, evaluating its overall fluidity, expressiveness, and resemblance to human speech.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Simultaneously, we utilize the \ac{SMOS to measure the perceived similarity between the speaker's voice in the input and the generated speech.}
elements.sent-2411-196: We engaged 20 listeners each evaluating clarity, reverberation accuracy, and naturalness on 6 samples of test set.
elements.sent-2411-197: Standard deviations across these scores were small (\textless0.25), indicating consistent evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-198: Table~\ref{tab:results presents the objective and subjective evaluation results across different models.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Compared to the baseline and prior scene-aware TTS systems (ViT-TTS and MS2KU-VTTS), our proposed method consistently achieves superior performance across nearly all metrics.From the objective perspective, our full model attains the lowest WER (7.6\%) and MCD (4.22), indicating enhanced intelligibility and spectral fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-200: Furthermore, it shows a notable improvement in SRE, reducing the mismatch between predicted and target scene acoustics to 27.2\%, a significant gain over ViT-TTS (64.2\%) and MS2KU-VTTS (57.1\%).
elements.sent-2411-201: The SECS also improves to 0.62, demonstrating better preservation of speaker identity.In terms of subjective quality, our model achieves the highest scores on NMOS (3.98), SMOS (4.21), and IMOS (3.96), reflecting improved naturalness, speaker similarity, and perceived scene-spatial alignment.
elements.sent-2411-202: Notably, even ablation variants (w/o CLIP or SR Classifier) outperform existing methods, highlighting the robustness of our architecture.These results confirm the effectiveness of integrating both visual scene prompts and speaker identity encoding into an end-to-end TTS framework, enabling the generation of immersive, high-quality, and scene-consistent reverberant speech.
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Experimental results for different environment.} \label{tab:environments} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{12pt} \setlength{\textwidth}{16pt} .85{ \begin{tabular}{c|cc|cc} ~ \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Wide environment} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Narrow environment} Model SRE(\%) $\downarrow$ IMOS $\uparrow$ SRE(\%) $\downarrow$ IMOS $\uparrow$ ViT-TTS 58.2 3.73 67.1 3.68 MS2KU-VTTS 54.7 3.89 62.8 3.81 Proposed 25.3 4.01 28.6 3.90 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-203: To further evaluate our model’s robustness across diverse acoustic conditions, we analyze performance separately in wide and narrow environments.
elements.sent-2411-204: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:environments , our proposed method significantly outperforms ViT-TTS and MS2KU-VTTS in both scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-205: In wide environments, which typically exhibit longer reverberation tails and more diffuse reflections, our model achieves the lowest Scene Reverb Error (SRE) at 25.3\% and the highest IMOS score of 4.01.
elements.sent-2411-206: This suggests that the model is more capable of capturing complex spatial cues from wide scenes, leading to more immersive and perceptually accurate reverberation.
elements.sent-2411-207: In narrow environments, which feature shorter reverberation times and tighter spatial constraints, our method again yields superior results with an SRE of 28.6\% and an IMOS of 3.90.
elements.sent-2411-208: Compared to the SREs of 67.1\% and 62.8\% from ViT-TTS and MS2KU-VTTS respectively, our approach demonstrates better adaptability and scene-awareness.
elements.sent-2411-209: These findings confirm that the integration of visual scene prompts and the refinement by the SRC module enable our model to generalize well across spatial contexts.
elements.sent-2411-210: The consistently higher IMOS scores also reflect that human listeners perceive the reverberation in our outputs to be more faithful to the corresponding visual scenes, regardless of environmental scale.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{gradcam.png} \caption{\ac{Grad-CAM}s for images passed the scene prompt encoder, showing movement towards more textured areas for (A) a wide, and (B) a narrow environment.} \label{fig:grad-cam}
elements.sent-2411-211: In this work, we presented a novel approach to end-to-end multi-modal \ac{TTS system that incorporates visual scene prompts to guide the generation of immersive, contextually appropriate speech.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Our method extends the capabilities of traditional \ac{TTS by introducing a framework that integrates spatial and scene-aware acoustic features into the synthesis process, addressing limitations in existing models.}
